criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the problem formulation: given a training set, test set, score function, and black-box task model, the goal is to find the optimal prompt that maximizes the expected score on the test set.

The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the Exemplar-Guided Reflection mechanism: an instructive meta-prompt guides the prompt optimizer to first select diverse, representative wrong samples as exemplars with detailed solution processes (chain-of-thought), and then generate feedbacks.

The agent has read Section 3.3 completely to understand the Feedback Memory mechanism: feedbacks are stored with priority scores, retrieved via softmax probability, and removed via a selective forgetting update rule.

The agent has read Section 3.4 completely to understand the Exemplar Factory mechanism: exemplars are stored with priority scores, retrieved using a combined priority-and-semantic-similarity score, and updated with a selective forgetting rule.
The agent has read Section 4 and Appendix A.1 to understand the seven evaluation datasets (WSC, ETHOS, ArSarcasm, LIAR, BBH-navigate, GSM8K, WebNLG) and their splits.

The agent has read Appendix A.2 to understand the implementation details: Doubao-Pro as the task model (temperature=0), GPT-4o-2024-05-13 as the prompt optimizer (temperature=1.0), 8 candidate prompts generated per step, and best-performing one selected for next iteration.

The agent has read Appendix E (Figures 4, 5, 6) to understand the three meta-prompts used: the instructive reflection meta-prompt, the optimization meta-prompt, and the retrieval optimization meta-prompt.
The agent has read Table 1 to check the main zero-shot comparison results across all seven datasets and eight methods.
The agent has read Tables 4, 5, and 6 to understand the ablation study results for each component of ERM (Exemplar-Guided Reflection, Feedback Memory, Exemplar Factory).
The plan includes the three-component architecture of ERM: Exemplar-Guided Reflection, Feedback Memory, and Exemplar Factory.

The plan includes implementing the instructive reflection meta-prompt that guides the optimizer to select exemplars with detailed chain-of-thought solutions from wrong samples before generating feedbacks.
The plan includes implementing Feedback Memory with storage, filtering, retrieval by priority score, and selective forgetting (priority score update and removal below threshold).
The plan includes implementing Exemplar Factory with storage, filtering (verify solution correctness and handle duplicates), retrieval by combined priority-semantic score, and selective forgetting.
The plan includes loading and splitting all seven datasets (LIAR, BBH-Navigate, ETHOS, ArSarcasm, WebNLG, GSM8K, WSC) with the correct train/test sizes as specified in Table 7.
The plan includes setting up Doubao-Pro as the task model and GPT-4o-2024-05-13 as the prompt optimizer with the specified temperatures.
The plan includes the beam search strategy for prompt selection: generating 8 candidate prompts per step and selecting the best-performing one on the validation set.
The plan includes implementing the six baseline methods for comparison: APE, ProTeGi, OPRO, Promptbreeder, EvoPrompt, and GPO.
The plan includes the evaluation metrics: F1 score for LIAR, BBH, ETHOS, ArSarcasm; Accuracy for WSC and GSM8K; ROUGE-L for WebNLG.
The plan includes running experiments under both zero-shot and few-shot settings, with five exemplars retrieved via kNN for the few-shot condition.
The plan includes ablation experiments testing each component of ERM independently (Exemplar-Guided Reflection only, with Exemplar Factory only, with Feedback Memory only, and all combined).
The plan includes running all experiments three times and reporting average results.
The code implements the prompt optimization objective as Equation (1): finding the optimal prompt p* that maximizes the expected score of the task model over the test set.

The code implements the instructive reflection meta-prompt (Figure 4) that instructs the prompt optimizer to identify N diverse typical wrong examples with step-by-step chain-of-thought solutions in JSON format wrapped in <key_example> tags, followed by N feedback reasons wrapped in <feedback> tags.

The code implements Equation (2): exemplar generation via the instructive meta-prompt, where the prompt optimizer selects n_e diverse and representative wrong samples from the wrong sample batch B and provides detailed solution processes for each.
The code implements Equation (3): feedback generation conditioned on the current prompt, wrong sample batch B, and the generated exemplars E^t, producing n_f feedback items.

The code implements Equation (4): prompt refinement where the optimizer produces a new prompt p^{t+1} based on the current prompt p^t, wrong samples B, and a single feedback f^t using the optimization meta-prompt.

The code implements the optimization meta-prompt (Figure 5) that provides the current prompt, wrong samples, a single feedback, and instructs the model to generate a refined prompt wrapped in <prompt> and </prompt> tags, with a word limit of 512 words.

The code implements the Feedback Memory storage with a filtering strategy: (1) only store feedbacks whose corresponding refined prompts bring improvements on the validation set, and (2) use BGE-M3 to compute semantic similarity and discard new feedbacks with high similarity to stored ones.
The code integrates the BGE-M3 model to compute semantic similarity between newly generated feedbacks and stored feedbacks for deduplication in Feedback Memory.
The code implements Equation (5): computing the selection probability for each stored feedback using softmax over exponential priority scores divided by temperature tau_f.
The code implements Equation (6): randomly sampling n_f_hat feedbacks from the stored feedbacks according to their selection probabilities.

The code implements the retrieval optimization meta-prompt (Figure 6) that provides the current prompt, wrong samples, and multiple retrieved feedbacks simultaneously to generate a refined prompt wrapped in <prompt> and </prompt> tags.

The code implements Equation (7): updating the priority score of each retrieved feedback using the formula s_p^t(f) = (1 - beta) * s_p(f)^{t-1} + beta * I(f), where I(f) indicates whether sufficient performance gain is achieved.
The code implements Equation (8): removing feedbacks from storage whose priority score falls below the threshold theta, retaining only feedbacks with s_p^t(f) >= theta.

The code implements the Exemplar Factory storage with a filtering strategy: (1) verify that the exemplar's chain-of-thought solution generated by the optimizer matches the ground-truth label, and (2) handle duplicate exemplars by replacing stored ones with probability p or rejecting the new one with probability 1-p.

The code implements Equation (9): computing the retrieval probability for each stored exemplar using softmax over the product of priority score s_p(e_i) and semantic similarity s_s^j(e_i) to the query question, divided by temperature tau_e.

The code implements the Exemplar Factory retrieval: during optimization, randomly sample 5 exemplars according to retrieval probabilities; during inference, deterministically select the 5 exemplars with the highest s_p(e_i) * s_s^j(e_i) scores.

The code implements the Exemplar Factory forgetting update: adjust exemplar priority scores based on whether incorporating them as variable content p_R(q_j) leads to performance improvement, and remove exemplars with low priority scores.

The code implements the full prompt optimization loop: evaluate task model on a subset of training set, collect wrong samples, apply Exemplar-Guided Reflection to generate exemplars and feedbacks, update Feedback Memory, generate refined prompts via beam search, evaluate candidates, and update scores.

The code implements the beam search strategy for prompt selection: generating 8 candidate prompts per optimization step and selecting the best-performing one on the validation set as the prompt for the next step.
The code implements the periodic retrieval of historical feedbacks from Feedback Memory (not every step) to generate new candidate prompts using the retrieval optimization meta-prompt.
The code sets up Doubao-Pro as the task model with temperature=0 for deterministic outputs.
The code sets up GPT-4o-2024-05-13 as the prompt optimizer with temperature=1.0 for diverse generation.
The code loads and preprocesses the LIAR dataset (3681 train, 461 test) following the same split as ProTeGi.
The code loads and preprocesses the BBH-Navigate dataset (96 train, 144 test) following the same split as GPO.
The code loads and preprocesses the ETHOS dataset (440 train, 200 test).
The code loads and preprocesses the ArSarcasm dataset (8437 train, 2110 test) using the original split.
The code loads and preprocesses the WebNLG dataset (200 train, 300 test) following the same split as GPO.
The code loads and preprocesses the GSM8K dataset (200 train, 300 test) following the same split as GPO.
The code loads and preprocesses the WSC dataset (100 train, 150 test) following the same split as GPO.
The code implements evaluation metrics: macro F1 score for LIAR, BBH-Navigate, ETHOS, ArSarcasm; accuracy for WSC and GSM8K; ROUGE-L for WebNLG.

The code implements the prompt composition structure where the full prompt p = [p_I, p_R(q_i)] can include an invariant task description part and a question-specific variable content part (exemplars).
The code implements the baseline methods APE and ProTeGi for comparison, including their respective meta-prompts and optimization procedures.
The code implements the baseline methods OPRO, Promptbreeder, EvoPrompt, and GPO for comparison.
The code implements the few-shot baseline where five relevant examples are dynamically selected via kNN clustering in the embedding space (for comparison in Table 3 and Table 10).
The code for the full ERM pipeline (Exemplar-Guided Reflection + Feedback Memory + Exemplar Factory) has been executed successfully without errors on all seven datasets under zero-shot setting.
The code for all baseline methods (APE, ProTeGi, OPRO, Promptbreeder, EvoPrompt, GPO) has been executed successfully without errors on all seven datasets under zero-shot setting.
The code for all methods including ERM has been executed successfully under few-shot setting on all seven datasets.
The code for all ablation configurations (Exemplar-Guided Reflection only, with Exemplar Factory only, with Feedback Memory only, and full ERM) has been executed successfully on all seven datasets.
The code for Exemplar Factory ablation (no retrieval, retrieval without filtering, retrieval with filtering, with selective forgetting) has been executed successfully on LIAR, BBH, and WebNLG.
The code for Feedback Memory ablation (no retrieval, retrieval without filtering, retrieval with filtering, with selective forgetting) has been executed successfully on LIAR, BBH, and WebNLG.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: ERM achieves F1=68.6 on LIAR, F1=86.1 on BBH, F1=98.0 on ETHOS, F1=85.1 on ArSarcasm, ROUGE-L=59.6 on WebNLG, Acc=93.3 on GSM8K, and Acc=86.0 on WSC under zero-shot setting.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: ERM outperforms the best baseline (ProTeGi) by +10.1 F1 on LIAR under zero-shot setting.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: ERM achieves the highest performance across all seven datasets compared to APE, ProTeGi, OPRO, Promptbreeder, EvoPrompt, and GPO under zero-shot setting.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: ERM outperforms all baseline methods under the few-shot setting across all seven datasets, with at least 8.3 F1 improvement over the best baseline on LIAR.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 4: adding Exemplar-Guided Reflection alone improves LIAR F1 from 58.5 (ProTeGi baseline) to 62.9; adding Exemplar Factory brings it to 67.2; adding Feedback Memory brings it to 66.6; combining all three reaches 68.6.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 5: exemplar retrieval without filtering slightly hurts performance (LIAR 62.3 vs 62.9); adding exemplar filtering improves LIAR to 65.7; adding selective forgetting further improves to 66.6.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 6: feedback retrieval without filtering slightly hurts performance (LIAR 66.4 vs 66.6); adding feedback filtering improves LIAR to 67.5; adding selective forgetting further improves to 68.6.
The execution outcomes match the efficiency results in Figure 3: ERM reaches peak performance on LIAR in 7 steps while ProTeGi requires 13 steps, demonstrating roughly half the optimization steps.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 9: ERM's total token consumption (approximately 7503 tokens) is comparable to non-feedback-based methods (APE: 7415, OPRO: 8652, GPO: 7360) despite being feedback-based, and significantly lower than ProTeGi (12836).

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 10: using ERM's Exemplar Factory exemplars (LIAR: 66.6, BBH: 82.6, WebNLG: 58.8) outperforms both zero-shot (62.9, 75.7, 56.9) and five relevant kNN examples (65.7, 78.5, 57.4).
The execution outcomes confirm that ERM-optimized prompts are more informative than ProTeGi-optimized prompts, as illustrated by the WebNLG prompt examples in Table 2.
